_meta.authoringNotes: Medical content reviewed against published literature on Paracoccidioides epidemiology in endemic regions (Latin America), IDSA guidelines on endemic mycoses, and UpToDate clinical resources.

h1Title: Pulmonary Paracoccidioidomycosis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Pulmonary paracoccidioidomycosis is a chronic fungal infection of the lungs caused by Paracoccidioides brasiliensis complex, found exclusively in the soil of Latin American forests — most prevalent in Brazil, Colombia, Venezuela, and Argentina. Inhalation of fungal spores from the environment leads to primary pulmonary infection in most cases, with the organism disseminating hematogenously to mucous membranes, skin, adrenal glands, and central nervous system over years. The disease is the tenth leading cause of infectious disease death in Brazil, with an estimated 80-90% of infections remaining asymptomatic initially. Antifungal therapy with itraconazole or sulfamethoxazole-trimethoprim is effective in most patients, though treatment courses are prolonged and relapses occur in 5-15% of cases after discontinuation.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 10-30 million people infected worldwide, with 80-90% of infections remaining asymptomatic (latent form); active disease incidence estimated at 1-3 per 100,000 in endemic areas
keyStats.demographics: Predominantly affects adult men aged 30-60 in rural agricultural occupations; male-to-female ratio in chronic form is approximately 13:1
keyStats.avgAge: Chronic paracoccidioidomycosis typically presents between ages 30-60; childhood (acute) form presents before age 15
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 10-30 million people infected in endemic regions of Latin America; Brazil accounts for approximately 80% of global cases

definition: Pulmonary paracoccidioidomycosis (ICD-10: B40.1) is a systemic endemic mycosis caused by Paracoccidioides species, a dimorphic fungus that lives as a mold in the environment and converts to a yeast in human tissues at body temperature. The organism is acquired by inhalation of conidia (spores) from soil in endemic rural areas, making the lungs the primary site of infection. The fungus produces a distinctive 'pilot's wheel' or 'ship's wheel' appearance in tissue — multiple budding yeast cells radiating from a central parent cell, pathognomonic for the disease. Acute (subacute) disseminated forms affect children and young adults with rapid onset of fever, lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, and bone marrow involvement resembling a hemophagocytic syndrome. Chronic forms develop after years of latency and primarily affect the lungs with fibrosis and pulmonary insufficiency, often accompanied by ulcerative mucosal lesions of the mouth, nose, and larynx. The disease is classified into acute (childhood), chronic (adult progressive), and a less common chronic localized form.

typesClassification.description: Rapid-onset form occurring primarily in children and young adults, characterized by widespread lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, bone marrow suppression, and systemic symptoms. This form represents active fungal dissemination and carries significant mortality if untreated. Also called the juvenile or childhood form.

The most common clinical form, representing approximately 75-80% of cases, typically presenting in adults aged 30-60 after years of latent infection. Combines pulmonary involvement with variable mucosal, cutaneous, adrenal, and CNS dissemination. Progressive fibrosis of the lungs is the leading cause of morbidity and death.

A less aggressive form in which disease is confined to a single organ or site — most commonly the oral or nasal mucosa (muggy stomatitis) or a single pulmonary lesion. Better prognosis than the disseminated form with fewer complications.

Fibrotic sequelae following treated or untreated pulmonary disease, including pulmonary fibrosis, bullous emphysema, bronchiectasis, and cor pulmonale. These structural changes persist after microbiologic cure and can cause progressive respiratory insufficiency.

primarySymptoms: Dry or productive cough, often chronic, present in 60-90% of pulmonary cases — reflecting the primary pulmonary site of Paracoccidioides infection.
Low-grade fever, weight loss, and night sweats — constitutional symptoms reflecting chronic fungal infection and inflammatory response, common in both acute and chronic forms.

Mucosal ulceration in the mouth, nose, or larynx — painful granulomatous ulcers with a characteristic 'strawberry gum' appearance (mulberry-like gingival enlargement), highly suggestive of the diagnosis.
Dyspnea on exertion and reduced exercise tolerance — resulting from progressive pulmonary fibrosis that destroys normal alveolar architecture and reduces gas exchange.
Hoarseness or voice changes — involvement of the laryngeal mucosa causes vocal cord inflammation and ulceration, leading to voice alterations that may progress to complete aphonia.

Skin lesions including papules, nodules, or verrucous plaques — distributed on the face, trunk, and extremities; the fungus reaches the skin through hematogenous spread from the primary pulmonary focus.

Adrenal insufficiency (Addison disease) — bilateral adrenal fungal infiltration is a major complication causing fatigue, hypotension, hyperpigmentation, and hyponatremia; occurs in 10-20% of disseminated cases.

Central nervous system involvement — headache, focal neurological deficits, and seizures from Paracoccidioides meningitis or parenchymal brain granulomas; occurs in 10-25% of chronic disseminated cases.
Lymphadenopathy (cervical, mediastinal, or generalized) — particularly prominent in the acute disseminated form, where lymph nodes may suppurate and drain, resembling tuberculous lymphadenitis.
Abdominal pain and hepatosplenomegaly — the liver and spleen are involved in disseminated disease; hepatic granulomas may cause cholestasis in severe cases.
earlyWarningSigns: Chronic cough lasting more than 4-6 weeks in a person with rural residence or agricultural occupation in Latin America
Mucosal ulcers of the mouth or nose that do not heal within 2-3 weeks, especially with a characteristic mulberry-like appearance
Unexplained weight loss, night sweats, and low-grade fever in a person from an endemic region with chronic pulmonary symptoms
Adrenal insufficiency with hyponatremia and hypotension in a patient from Latin America without prior TB history
Brain granuloma or meningitis in a patient from an endemic country — Paracoccidioides CNS infection is an important differential diagnosis for chronic meningitis in Latin America
emergencySigns: Acute respiratory distress with severe hypoxemia (PaO2 below 60 mmHg) — end-stage pulmonary fibrosis or acute respiratory distress syndrome-like presentation in advanced untreated disease
Meningeal signs (nuchal rigidity, photophobia, severe headache) — Paracoccidioides meningitis has a subacute-to-chronic course and can cause hydrocephalus and cranial nerve palsies
Gastrointestinal bleeding from esophageal or gastric ulceration — extensive mucosal disease can erode major vessels
Adrenal crisis with severe hypotension, hyponatremia, and shock — requires immediate corticosteroid replacement in addition to antifungal therapy
Seizures or altered mental status from CNS parenchymal involvement — brain granulomas can cause mass effect, edema, and epilepsy

causes.cause: Inhalation of Paracoccidioides conidia from soil
causes.description: The primary route of infection — airborne conidia (1-5 micrometers) are inhaled from the soil of endemic rural forests, reaching the alveoli where they convert to the invasive yeast form. The ecological niche is acidic, nutrient-rich forest soil — particularly in coffee-growing and sugar-cane agricultural areas. Infection typically occurs during childhood exposure during farming activities.

In 90% of infected individuals, the immune system contains but does not eradicate the fungus — it remains dormant in pulmonary granulomas for years or decades. Immunosuppression (corticosteroid use, HIV/AIDS, chemotherapy) or declining cell-mediated immunity can trigger reactivation and dissemination.

HIV co-infection dramatically increases risk of disseminated paracoccidioidomycosis — CD4 counts below 200 cells/microliter are associated with aggressive dissemination and higher mortality. Systemic corticosteroids suppress the Th1 immune response required to contain Paracoccidioides in granulomas.

Direct occupational exposure to soil — farming (especially coffee, sugarcane, cotton, and tobacco cultivation), forestry, and mining work in endemic areas is the strongest epidemiological risk factor. Farmers who work with soil disruption in forest areas have the highest infection rates.

Chronic alcoholism and malnutrition — both common in the rural poor of endemic regions — impair cell-mediated immunity and allow fungal dissemination. Patients with paracoccidioidomycosis in Brazil have a markedly higher prevalence of alcoholism than matched controls.

causes.cause: Geographical residence or travel to endemic regions
causes.description: Permanent residence in or extensive travel to areas of Central and South America where the fungus is endemic — particularly Brazil (nationwide), Colombia, Venezuela, Ecuador, Peru, and northern Argentina. Short-term tourists have very low risk; disease is linked to prolonged exposure.

riskFactors.factor: Rural residence in endemic Latin American regions
riskFactors.description: Residence in or long-term exposure to endemic rural areas of Central and South America is the dominant risk factor. The fungus thrives in acidic, humid forest soils — particularly in areas with disrupted vegetation.

Chronic paracoccidioidomycosis shows a striking male predominance (13:1 male-to-female ratio). Estrogen is believed to inhibit Paracoccidioides yeast-phase conversion, providing women with partial natural protection.
Farming, forestry, and mining activities in endemic areas expose workers to soil aerosolization and are strongly associated with infection and active disease.
Chronic alcoholism is the most significant comorbidity risk factor for paracoccidioidomycosis in Brazil — it impairs macrophage and T-cell function, facilitating fungal dissemination.

Immunosuppressed individuals, particularly those with HIV/AIDS (CD4 below 200), are at dramatically elevated risk for disseminated disease. Antiretroviral therapy significantly reduces incidence in co-infected populations.
Protein-calorie malnutrition, common among rural poor populations, impairs cell-mediated immunity — the primary host defense against Paracoccidioides — and is associated with more severe disease.
Long-term systemic corticosteroid therapy suppresses Th1 cytokine responses and granuloma integrity, predisposing to reactivation and dissemination of latent Paracoccidioides infection.

affectedDemographics: Brazil accounts for approximately 80% of global cases, with highest endemicity in the southeastern states (Sao Paulo, Minas Gerais, Rio de Janeiro) and Amazonian regions
Colombia, Venezuela, Ecuador, Peru, Bolivia, and northern Argentina report endemic paracoccidioidomycosis with regional variation in strain genetics
Adult men aged 30-60 in rural occupations represent the majority of chronic cases — the male predominance is one of the most striking demographic features of the disease
Women and children have lower incidence of active disease due to estrogen-mediated inhibition of the yeast phase and more limited occupational soil exposure, respectively
Outside Latin America, paracoccidioidomycosis is seen almost exclusively in immigrants from endemic countries or, rarely, in laboratory-acquired infections
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of pulmonary paracoccidioidomycosis requires a high index of suspicion in patients from or with travel history to endemic Latin American regions with compatible respiratory or systemic symptoms. The diagnosis is established by direct visualization of the characteristic 'pilot's wheel' yeast form (multiple budding cells radiating from a central parent cell) in clinical specimens. Sputum cytology (induced sputum or bronchoscopy with bronchoalveolar lavage) is the most practical initial diagnostic test — sensitivity is approximately 60-85% with direct microscopy. Histopathology of lung, mucosal, or skin biopsy shows the pathognomonic multi-budding yeast form, and biopsy is indicated when sputum sampling is non-diagnostic. Serology (immunodiffusion, counterimmunoelectrophoresis, ELISA) detects anti-Paracoccidioides antibodies in 85-95% of active cases — falling titers are used to monitor treatment response. Antigen detection (using the galactomannan-like antigen) is available in reference labs and is particularly useful in severely immunocompromised patients who may not mount an antibody response. Chest CT typically shows a combination of alveolar infiltrates, nodules, cavitary lesions, and traction bronchiectasis in the chronic fibrocavitary form. Culture on Sabouraud agar takes 2-6 weeks and is primarily used for species identification and antifungal susceptibility testing rather than acute diagnosis.

diagnosticTests.test: Direct microscopy of sputum or BAL (KOH preparation)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Rapid, low-cost first-line diagnostic test. Induced sputum or bronchoalveolar lavage fluid is examined under KOH microscopy — the pathognomonic multi-budding yeast with 'pilot's wheel' morphology confirms the diagnosis within hours. Sensitivity 60-85% in expectorated sputum with serial sampling.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Non-invasive sputum collection (patient coughs after nebulized saline induction) or bronchoscopy if sputum is non-diagnostic. Results available within 1-2 hours of specimen receipt in the laboratory.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects IgG antibodies against Paracoccidioides in 85-95% of patients with active disease. Immunodiffusion (ID) and counterimmunoelectrophoresis (CIE) are traditional reference standards; ELISA provides quantitative titers. High or rising titers indicate active infection; falling titers track treatment response.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw in any clinical laboratory. Results from reference mycology labs in 3-7 days. Serial titers every 3-6 months track response to therapy — a four-fold titer decline indicates treatment success.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses the extent and pattern of pulmonary involvement — distinguishes acute alveolar infiltrates from the chronic fibrocavitary form, guides treatment intensity, and provides a baseline for monitoring fibrosis progression.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard chest CT, 15-30 minutes, no contrast required for pulmonary parenchymal assessment. Results available within 1-2 days.

diagnosticTests.test: Lung or mucosal biopsy with histopathology
diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold standard diagnostic test when sputum is non-diagnostic. Tissue sections show the pathognomonic multi-budding Paracoccidioides yeast with GMS (Grocott methenamine silver) or PAS staining.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Bronchoscopic lung biopsy or punch biopsy of mucosal/skin lesion under local anesthetic. Results in 3-5 days. Tissue is also sent for culture.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms the diagnosis and allows species identification (Paracoccidioides americana vs. P. brasiliensis complex) and antifungal susceptibility testing. Culture takes 2-6 weeks — it is adjunctive to rapid methods.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Sputum, BAL, or tissue biopsy inoculated onto fungal culture media. Mycology reference labs report results in 2-6 weeks. Positive culture confirms viability of the organism.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects circulating galactomannan antigen in serum — useful in immunocompromised patients who may have false-negative serology and in monitoring treatment response (antigen levels fall with effective therapy).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw sent to reference mycology laboratory. Results in 1-2 weeks.

diagnosticTests.test: HIV testing (for all patients with paracoccidioidomycosis)
diagnosticTests.purpose: HIV co-infection dramatically alters management — co-infected patients require concurrent ART and antifungal therapy, higher antifungal doses, and longer treatment. All patients diagnosed with paracoccidioidomycosis should be tested for HIV.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw for HIV antibody and antigen testing. Results in 1-2 days.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of pulmonary paracoccidioidomycosis (South American blastomycosis) caused by Paracoccidioides brasiliensis and related species requires prolonged antifungal therapy. The first-line agent is itraconazole 200 mg orally twice daily for 9 to 12 months, which achieves cure rates above 80 percent and is superior to older regimens in terms of efficacy and tolerability. Serum itraconazole levels should be monitored to ensure adequate drug exposure. Alternatives include sulfamethoxazole-trimethoprim at full doses (sulfamethoxazole 800 mg plus trimethoprim 160 mg twice daily) for 9 to 12 months, which is effective but has higher rates of adverse effects including hypersensitivity and bone marrow suppression. Severe or acute presentations (the progressive acute form) require initial treatment with amphotericin B deoxycholate 0.7 to 1 mg per kg IV daily until clinical improvement, typically 2 to 4 weeks, then transition to oral itraconazole for maintenance therapy. Voriconazole or posaconazole are reserved for refractory cases or intolerance to first-line agents. Adjunctive corticosteroids may be required for severe pulmonary inflammation or disseminated disease with adrenal involvement. Antifungal treatment is continued until clinical and radiologic remission and negative follow-up sputum cultures are achieved. Relapse occurs in 10 to 15 percent of cases after treatment completion; maintenance therapy with itraconazole for 1 to 2 additional years reduces relapse risk. Serial chest imaging and clinical assessment guide treatment duration. Patient education about treatment adherence, monitoring for complications, and recognizing signs of disease progression is essential. Shared decision-making between the patient and treatment team improves outcomes and quality of life. Regular follow-up visits allow assessment of treatment response, management of adverse effects, and adjustment of therapy as needed. Multidisciplinary involvement including specialist physicians, nursing staff, social workers, and mental health professionals optimizes comprehensive care delivery.

medicalTreatments.name: Itraconazole oral solution 200 mg twice daily
medicalTreatments.description: First-line treatment for mild-to-moderate chronic pulmonary paracoccidioidomycosis. Oral itraconazole is effective in 80-95% of cases with a favorable side effect profile. Treatment duration is 6-12 months, extended to 12-18 months for more severe or disseminated cases. Must be taken with a full meal for optimal absorption; oral solution has better bioavailability than capsules.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rates of 80-95% in non-disseminated pulmonary disease after 6-12 months of therapy; relapse rate approximately 5-15% after treatment discontinuation.

medicalTreatments.name: Sulfamethoxazole-trimethoprim (SMX-TMP) 800/160 mg twice daily
medicalTreatments.description: Widely used first-line alternative, particularly in resource-limited settings across Latin America. Effective for both pulmonary and disseminated disease including CNS involvement due to superior CNS penetration. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole is inexpensive and widely available. Treatment duration 6-24 months. Side effects include rash, cytopenias, and hyperkalemia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical response rates equivalent to itraconazole (85-95%) in mild-to-moderate disease; superior for CNS disease due to CNS penetration; widely used in endemic countries as first-line therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Amphotericin B deoxycholate or liposomal amphotericin B (3-5 mg/kg/day IV)
medicalTreatments.description: Reserved for severe acute disseminated disease, rapidly progressive pulmonary disease with respiratory failure, or adrenal crisis. Amphotericin B rapidly reduces fungal burden in critical illness. Liposomal formulation (3-5 mg/kg) reduces nephrotoxicity compared to conventional deoxycholate (0.7-1 mg/kg/day). Transition to oral azole or SMX-TMP after 2-4 weeks of intravenous therapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Rapid clinical response in severe disease within days to weeks; essential for acute life-threatening dissemination; transition to oral therapy required due to cumulative toxicity.

medicalTreatments.name: Voriconazole or posaconazole (for azole-refractory disease)
medicalTreatments.description: Broad-spectrum triazoles used in patients who fail or cannot tolerate first-line therapy. Posaconazole has excellent activity against Paracoccidioides in vitro and is increasingly used for refractory cases. Requires therapeutic drug monitoring (voriconazole trough levels 2-5 mg/L).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Case series demonstrate efficacy in azole-refractory paracoccidioidomycosis; posaconazole and voriconazole show good activity in vitro and in animal models.

medicalTreatments.name: Systemic corticosteroids (adjunctive, short-term)
medicalTreatments.description: Short courses of oral prednisone (0.5-1 mg/kg/day for 2-4 weeks with taper) are used in immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS) after ART initiation in HIV co-infected patients, and for severe pulmonary inflammation causing respiratory compromise.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Adjunctive benefit in select patients with severe inflammatory pulmonary infiltrates or IRIS; corticosteroids alone without antifungal therapy are contraindicated.

medicalTreatments.name: Adrenal hormone replacement (hydrocortisone + fludrocortisone)
medicalTreatments.description: Patients with adrenal insufficiency from Paracoccidioides adrenal infiltration require lifelong glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid replacement — hydrocortisone 10-20 mg morning / 5-10 mg afternoon and fludrocortisone 0.05-0.2 mg daily. This is critical in Addisonian patients, especially during stress (infection, surgery).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Lifelong adrenal replacement restores quality of life and prevents adrenal crisis in patients with established adrenal insufficiency — must be continued even after antifungal cure.

surgicalOptions.name: Tracheostomy for laryngeal obstruction
surgicalOptions.description: Severe laryngeal stenosis and airway obstruction from mucosal-paracoccidioidomycosis may require temporary or permanent tracheostomy to maintain airway patency. Indicated when progressive ulcerative laryngitis threatens airway compromise.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Life-saving airway intervention in severe laryngeal obstruction; combined with antifungal therapy to control the underlying fungal infection.

surgicalOptions.name: Endoscopic airway dilation
surgicalOptions.description: For subglottic stenosis from chronic laryngeal involvement, serial endoscopic dilation may be used as an alternative to tracheostomy in selected patients.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Variable results — scarring may recur after dilation; combined with antifungal therapy to reduce inflammatory stimulus.

alternativeTreatments.name: Ketoconazole 400 mg daily
alternativeTreatments.description: An older azole antifungal with proven efficacy in paracoccidioidomycosis. Less preferred than itraconazole due to hepatotoxicity and inhibition of testosterone/cortisol synthesis at high doses. Treatment duration 6-12 months.

alternativeTreatments.name: Iodide therapy (saturated solution of potassium iodide, SSKI)
alternativeTreatments.description: Historical treatment used adjunctively in the pre-azola era — potassium iodide was used topically and systemically for mucosal and cutaneous lesions. No longer recommended as monotherapy but occasionally used as adjunct in refractory mucosal disease.

whySeeSpecialist: All patients with paracoccidioidomycosis should be managed by an infectious disease specialist with experience in endemic mycoses. Pulmonary involvement requires evaluation by a pulmonologist for fibrosis staging and management of respiratory complications. HIV co-infected patients need concurrent management with an HIV specialist. Adrenal insufficiency requires endocrinology referral. Laryngeal or CNS involvement may require otolaryngology or neurology referral, respectively. Management in an endemic country with access to itraconazole or SMX-TMP and mycology labs is optimal.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek an infectious disease specialist or a physician with documented experience treating endemic mycoses in Latin American populations. Ask whether they use itraconazole or SMX-TMP as first-line therapy, whether they monitor treatment response with serial serology, and whether they have access to mycology reference labs for culture and antifungal susceptibility. For pulmonary complications, a pulmonologist familiar with fibrocavitary lung disease is essential.
preventionStrategies: There is no effective vaccine against Paracoccidioides — prevention focuses on reducing exposure in endemic rural areas through use of respiratory protection (N95 masks) during high-risk agricultural activities
Early detection and treatment of latent infection in high-risk individuals (rural workers with positive serology) may prevent progression to active disease, analogous to TB preventive therapy
Antiretroviral therapy in HIV patients from endemic areas prevents the severe disseminated form — HIV testing and linkage to ART is a population-level preventive measure
Reducing alcoholism and malnutrition in endemic populations — both major risk factors for disseminated disease — through public health programs
lifestyleModifications: Avoid exposure to disturbed forest soil in endemic regions — agricultural workers should use respiratory protection (N95 mask) when plowing, planting, or harvesting in areas with known endemic paracoccidioidomycosis
Eliminate or reduce alcohol consumption — alcoholism accelerates fungal dissemination and impairs treatment response; patients with alcoholism-related paracoccidioidomycosis have worse outcomes

Maintain adequate nutrition — protein-calorie sufficiency supports cell-mediated immunity and improves response to antifungal therapy; nutritional supplementation is particularly important in underweight patients
Adhere to long-term antifungal therapy — premature discontinuation is the most common cause of relapse; complete the full prescribed course even after symptoms improve
Report any adrenal symptoms (fatigue, lightheadedness, salt craving) promptly — adrenal insufficiency may emerge during or after treatment and requires urgent assessment

recommended: High-protein diet (1.2-1.5 g/kg/day) — supports T-cell immunity, muscle mass, and recovery from chronic fungal infection; includes lean meats, fish, legumes, and dairy
Iron-rich foods (lean red meat, fortified cereals, legumes) — patients with chronic disease often have anemia of chronic disease; iron supports hemoglobin and immune cell production
Zinc and selenium supplementation if deficient — trace elements critical for lymphocyte function; zinc deficiency impairs cell-mediated immunity
Vitamin D 1000-2000 IU daily — supports immune regulation; patients with limited sun exposure (chronic illness) are at risk for deficiency
Frequent small meals if appetite is poor — chronic infection causes anorexia; 5-6 smaller meals daily helps maintain caloric intake
avoid: Raw or undercooked foods in patients with adrenal insufficiency on replacement therapy — adrenal patients are immunocompromised and at risk for foodborne infection
Grapefruit and grapefruit juice — inhibits CYP3A4 and reduces itraconazole absorption, potentially reducing antifungal efficacy
Alcohol — alcohol impairs macrophage function, worsens adrenal insufficiency, and accelerates liver disease progression; contraindicated during and after treatment
Excessive dairy if patient has concurrent lactose intolerance — GI symptoms distract from nutritional goals

exerciseGuidelines: Exercise is generally limited by pulmonary symptoms (dyspnea, fatigue) in active disease. Light walking (15-30 minutes daily) is encouraged when oxygen saturation permits. Strenuous exercise is avoided in patients with active pulmonary disease or adrenal insufficiency until medically stable. Once antifungal therapy is completed and pulmonary function has stabilized, a gradual return to normal exercise capacity is appropriate with cardiologist or pulmonologist guidance for patients with established fibrosis.
dailyManagement: Take itraconazole oral solution with a full meal (full-fat meal optimizes absorption) or SMX-TMP as prescribed — adherence to the full course of therapy is the single most important factor in cure
Schedule and attend all follow-up appointments — serial serology (every 3-6 months) and periodic chest imaging are needed to confirm treatment response and detect relapse early
Monitor for itraconazole side effects — hepatotoxicity (fatigue, nausea, dark urine), heart failure exacerbation (itraconazole can worsen HF), and drug interactions (many azoles inhibit CYP3A4)
Report any adrenal symptoms (severe fatigue, dizziness, salt craving, hyperpigmentation) immediately — adrenal insufficiency may develop during treatment and requires urgent evaluation
Take itraconazole capsules or solution separately from antacids, H2 blockers, and proton pump inhibitors — these reduce gastric acidity and impair itraconazole dissolution and absorption
prognosis: With appropriate antifungal therapy, pulmonary paracoccidioidomycosis has a favorable prognosis — cure rates of 80-95% are reported with 6-12 months of itraconazole or SMX-TMP for non-disseminated disease. The prognosis is determined by the extent of disease at diagnosis and the immune status of the host. Patients with limited pulmonary disease treated early have normal life expectancy. Disseminated disease with adrenal insufficiency, CNS involvement, or severe pulmonary fibrosis carries a worse prognosis — adrenal insufficiency is permanent in most cases, requiring lifelong hormone replacement. HIV co-infected patients have higher mortality, particularly those with CD4 counts below 200, even with antifungal therapy. Relapse rates of 5-15% after treatment cessation underscore the importance of post-treatment surveillance. Patients with established pulmonary fibrosis may have residual respiratory insufficiency even after microbiologic cure.
recoveryTimeline: Clinical response typically begins within 4-8 weeks of effective antifungal therapy — fever resolves, constitutional symptoms improve, and weight gain begins. Mucosal ulcers begin to epithelialize within 2-4 weeks. Pulmonary infiltrates on imaging gradually clear over 3-6 months. Serology titers fall progressively — a four-fold decline at 3-6 months is an early indicator of treatment success. Most patients complete 6-12 months of therapy with documented clinical improvement and serologic decline. Full radiographic clearance of fibrotic lesions is uncommon — residual scarring persists after cure. Relapse surveillance continues for 1-2 years after treatment discontinuation.
complications: Pulmonary fibrosis and respiratory insufficiency — chronic pulmonary disease destroys alveolar architecture, causing restrictive lung disease and progressive dyspnea; cor pulmonale from chronic hypoxia is the most common cause of death in treated patients

Adrenal insufficiency (Addison disease) — bilateral adrenal infiltration by Paracoccidioides causes permanent cortisol and aldosterone deficiency in 10-20% of disseminated cases; requires lifelong glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid replacement

CNS paracoccidioidomycosis (meningitis, brain granulomas) — occurs in 10-25% of chronic disseminated cases; causes headache, seizures, cranial nerve palsies, and hydrocephalus; mortality is high without treatment and survivors often have neurological sequelae
Laryngeal stenosis — chronic ulcerative laryngitis can cause progressive airway narrowing and dysphonia; severe cases may require tracheostomy
Cor pulmonale (right heart failure) — chronic hypoxemia from pulmonary fibrosis causes pulmonary hypertension and right ventricular failure; indicates advanced pulmonary disease
Malnutrition and cachexia — chronic infection with anorexia and hypermetabolism causes severe protein-calorie malnutrition, particularly in patients with alcoholism; worsens immune function

supportResources.name: Sociedade Brasileira de Infectologia — Paracoccidioidomicose Guidelines
supportResources.description: Brazilian Infectious Diseases Society clinical guideline on paracoccidioidomycosis management — the definitive Portuguese-language clinical reference.

supportResources.name: IDSA Endemic Mycoses Guidelines — Paracoccidioidomycosis Chapter
supportResources.description: Infectious Diseases Society of America guidelines on endemic mycoses including paracoccidioidomycosis — US clinical reference with evidence-based treatment recommendations.

supportResources.name: PAHO Paracoccidioidomycosis Technical Notes
supportResources.description: Pan American Health Organization technical notes on surveillance, diagnosis, and management of paracoccidioidomycosis in Latin America.

supportResources.name: Wanke B, et al. Paracoccidioidomycosis. Expert Rev Anti Infect Ther. 2021.
supportResources.description: Comprehensive review of paracoccidioidomycosis pathogenesis, diagnosis, and management with focus on the Brazilian endemic context.

supportResources.name: Travassos LR, Taborda CP. Paracoccidioidomycosis: Advances in treatment and prophylaxis. J Fungi (Basel). 2022.
supportResources.description: Review of current antifungal treatment strategies and experimental prophylactic approaches for paracoccidioidomycosis.

supportResources.name: Shikanai-Yasuda MA, et al. Brazilian guidelines for the clinical management of paracoccidioidomycosis. Rev Soc Bras Med Trop. 2017.
supportResources.description: The definitive Brazilian guideline on paracoccidioidomycosis — covers diagnosis, treatment protocols, and public health aspects of the disease.

confusedWithConditions.name: Tuberculosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: TB and paracoccidioidomycosis share chronic cough, constitutional symptoms, cavitary lung lesions, and endemic geography that overlaps in Latin America. AFB smear/culture differentiates TB; Paracoccidioides shows the characteristic multi-budding yeast form in KOH prep. Both can coexist.

confusedWithConditions.name: Histoplasmosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Histoplasma capsulatum causes similar pulmonary and disseminated disease in the Americas, but tissue forms differ — Histoplasma shows small intracellular yeasts within macrophages, while Paracoccidioides shows the pathognomonic multi-budding 'pilot's wheel' form. Serology and culture distinguish the two.

confusedWithConditions.name: COPD
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Chronic paracoccidioidomycosis pulmonary fibrosis can mimic COPD on imaging, but COPD sputum culture is negative and there is no exposure history. COPD patients lack the mucosal ulceration and constitutional symptoms typical of disseminated paracoccidioidomycosis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Blastomycosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Blastomyces dermatitidis causes similar pulmonary mycosis in North America but is rare in Latin America. Histology of Blastomyces shows single broad-based budding yeast (not the multi-budding 'pilot's wheel' of Paracoccidioides). Geographic history is the key differentiator.

relatedConditions.name: Histoplasmosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Both Paracoccidioides and Histoplasma are dimorphic endemic fungi causing similar pulmonary and disseminated disease; geographic and histopathologic features distinguish them. Some clinical features and antifungal treatment strategies overlap.

relatedConditions.name: Blastomycosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Blastomyces dermatitidis shares the dimorphic fungal biology and pulmonary primary infection pattern with Paracoccidioides; differential diagnosis requires awareness of distinct geographic distributions.

relatedConditions.name: Coccidioidomycosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Coccidioides immitis/posadasii is another dimorphic endemic fungus with a primary pulmonary entry point, spherule tissue form, and similar geographic restriction pattern to Paracoccidioides — both require azole therapy.

relatedConditions.name: Tuberculosis
relatedConditions.relevance: TB is the most important differential diagnosis and co-infection partner in endemic Latin American regions — both cause cavitary lung disease, weight loss, and night sweats. Co-infection occurs in 5-10% of paracoccidioidomycosis cases in high-burden TB areas.

relatedConditions.name: HIV/AIDS
relatedConditions.relevance: HIV/AIDS dramatically increases risk of disseminated paracoccidioidomycosis; all patients with confirmed paracoccidioidomycosis should be tested for HIV. Concurrent ART and antifungal therapy improves survival in co-infected patients.

faqs.question: What is pulmonary paracoccidioidomycosis?
faqs.answer: Pulmonary paracoccidioidomycosis is a chronic fungal lung infection caused by Paracoccidioides brasiliensis complex — a dimorphic fungus endemic to the soil of Latin American forests. It is acquired by inhaling spores from contaminated soil and is the leading systemic endemic mycosis in Central and South America.

faqs.question: Is paracoccidioidomycosis contagious?
faqs.answer: No — paracoccidioidomycosis is not transmitted person-to-person. It is acquired exclusively from the environment (inhaling spores from soil). Person-to-person transmission has never been documented. Patients do not need isolation precautions.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of paracoccidioidomycosis?
faqs.answer: Symptoms include chronic cough, weight loss, low-grade fever, night sweats, and in disseminated disease — painful oral/nasal ulcers, hoarseness, skin lesions, and in severe cases, adrenal insufficiency or brain involvement.

faqs.question: How is paracoccidioidomycosis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: The diagnosis is confirmed by visualizing the characteristic 'pilot's wheel' multi-budding yeast in sputum, bronchoalveolar lavage fluid, or tissue biopsy under KOH preparation or histopathology. Serology (antibody tests) and fungal culture are supportive. Chest CT shows the pattern of pulmonary involvement.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for paracoccidioidomycosis?
faqs.answer: First-line treatment is oral itraconazole 200 mg twice daily for 6-12 months, or sulfamethoxazole-trimethoprim (SMX-TMP) twice daily for 6-24 months. Severe disseminated disease requires initial IV amphotericin B. Treatment courses are prolonged because the fungus is difficult to eradicate from granulomas.

faqs.question: Is paracoccidioidomycosis curable?
faqs.answer: Yes — with 6-18 months of appropriate antifungal therapy, 80-95% of patients achieve clinical cure. However, residual pulmonary fibrosis may persist even after microbiologic cure. Relapse occurs in 5-15% of patients after stopping therapy, requiring surveillance.

faqs.question: Where is paracoccidioidomycosis found?
faqs.answer: Paracoccidioidomycosis is restricted to Latin America. Brazil accounts for approximately 80% of global cases, followed by Colombia, Venezuela, Ecuador, Peru, Bolivia, and Argentina. The fungus lives as a mold in humid acidic forest soils — particularly in rural agricultural areas.

faqs.question: Why does paracoccidioidomycosis affect more men than women?
faqs.answer: Adult men are affected 10-15 times more frequently than women. Estrogen is believed to inhibit the conversion of Paracoccidioides from its mold (environmental) form to the invasive yeast form in human tissues, providing women with natural partial protection. Occupational soil exposure in men (agricultural work) also contributes.

faqs.question: Can paracoccidioidomycosis spread to the brain?
faqs.answer: Yes — CNS involvement occurs in 10-25% of chronic disseminated cases, manifesting as Paracoccidioides meningitis or brain parenchymal granulomas. Symptoms include headache, focal neurological deficits, and seizures. CNS disease requires long-term therapy with drugs that penetrate the blood-brain barrier (SMX-TMP is preferred).

faqs.question: Does paracoccidioidomycosis affect the adrenal glands?
faqs.answer: Yes — bilateral adrenal infiltration is a major complication of disseminated disease, occurring in 10-20% of cases. It causes Addison disease (adrenal insufficiency) with fatigue, hypotension, hyperpigmentation, and hyponatremia. Adrenal insufficiency is often permanent, requiring lifelong hormone replacement even after antifungal cure.

faqs.question: How long does treatment for paracoccidioidomycosis take?
faqs.answer: Treatment of paracoccidioidomycosis is prolonged — typically 6-12 months of itraconazole or SMX-TMP for non-disseminated pulmonary disease, extending to 12-24 months for severe disseminated or CNS disease. Shorter courses are associated with high relapse rates.

faqs.question: What is the difference between paracoccidioidomycosis and histoplasmosis?
faqs.answer: Both are dimorphic endemic fungi causing pulmonary and disseminated disease. The key distinguishing feature is the tissue form: Histoplasma appears as small (2-4 micrometer) intracellular yeasts within macrophages, while Paracoccidioides shows the pathognomonic multi-budding 'pilot's wheel' pattern with multiple daughter cells radiating from a parent cell. Their geographic distributions also differ — Histoplasma is primarily North American while Paracoccidioides is Latin American.

faqs.question: Is there a vaccine for paracoccidioidomycosis?
faqs.answer: No — there is no approved vaccine for paracoccidioidomycosis. Prevention focuses on reducing soil exposure in endemic regions using respiratory protection and controlling HIV/AIDS to prevent severe disseminated disease in endemic populations.

faqs.question: Can paracoccidioidomycosis come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Yes — relapse occurs in 5-15% of patients after completing treatment, particularly in immunocompromised individuals, those with residual pulmonary fibrosis, or patients who received insufficient therapy duration. Long-term clinical surveillance with serial serology is recommended for 1-2 years after treatment completion.

faqs.question: What happens if paracoccidioidomycosis is left untreated?
faqs.answer: Untreated chronic pulmonary paracoccidioidomycosis progresses to widespread fibrosis, destroying lung architecture and causing respiratory insufficiency and cor pulmonale (right heart failure). Disseminated disease causes adrenal destruction, CNS involvement, and death within 5-10 years in most untreated patients.

faqs.question: Should family members of a patient with paracoccidioidomycosis be treated?
faqs.answer: No — paracoccidioidomycosis is not contagious person-to-person, so household contacts do not require treatment unless they have symptoms. Family members with relevant exposure history (rural residence in endemic regions) should be screened if symptomatic.

faqs.question: Does paracoccidioidomycosis affect the lungs only?
faqs.answer: No — while the lungs are the primary site of infection, the fungus spreads hematogenously to multiple organs over time. Disseminated disease affects the oral and nasal mucosa (ulcerative mucositis), skin, adrenal glands (causing Addison disease), lymph nodes, liver, and central nervous system. Most deaths are from pulmonary fibrosis or CNS disease.

faqs.question: What is the 'pilot's wheel' appearance in paracoccidioidomycosis?
faqs.answer: The 'pilot's wheel' or 'ship's wheel' is the pathognomonic tissue form of Paracoccidioides — a large yeast cell (10-60 micrometers) surrounded by multiple small daughter cells budding radially in a circumferential pattern. This distinctive morphology, visible in tissue biopsies under GMS or PAS stain, confirms the diagnosis and distinguishes it from all other fungal infections.

faqs.question: Can I take itraconazole with food?
faqs.answer: Itraconazole capsules must be taken with a full meal (food enhances dissolution and absorption), but the oral solution should be taken on an empty stomach (or with a light meal). Taking capsules with antacids, PPIs, or H2 blockers reduces absorption significantly and should be avoided. Grapefruit juice also reduces itraconazole levels.

symptomKeywords: chronic cough weight loss endemic mycosis
